coredata.dc:title: Multivalent manganese-based composite materials for sodium energy storage in ether electrolyte

coredata.dc:description: The structural collapse of manganese-based materials during cycling greatly restricts its development in Sodium-ion batteries (SIBs). Hence, two-phase structures with different manganese valence states are designed based on an alternating voltage approach to mitigate volume expansion via two-phase coordination. Here distinct manganese valence states of precursor are driven via atmosphere. And in the following heat-induced phase transition, the elemental valence state is the decisive key for the structural and compositional evolution. In a batch of synthetic materials, MnO/MnS with bivalent states of manganese performs best as expected. Furthermore, NaPF6 in DEGDME is taken to replace the traditional carbonate electrolyte which is prone to produce unstable SEI film and leading to polarization. A series of characterization methods confirm that the energy barrier of charge transfer at the interface between electrolyte and electrode is the main factor determining the electrochemical characteristics of the interface and thus the energy storage performance. Ultimately, carbon dots (CDS) with rich functional groups are introduced in an effort to further promote the conductivity of MnO/MnS, and the in-situ generated C-S-Mn bond enhances electrochemical kinetic behaviors. This work provides an approach for designing manganese-based materials and the selection of electrolytes in sodium energy storage.

head.ce:title: Multivalent manganese-based composite materials for sodium energy storage in ether electrolyte

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The structural collapse of manganese-based materials during cycling greatly restricts its development in Sodium-ion batteries (SIBs). Hence, two-phase structures with different manganese valence states are designed based on an alternating voltage approach to mitigate volume expansion via two-phase coordination. Here distinct manganese valence states of precursor are driven via atmosphere. And in the following heat-induced phase transition, the elemental valence state is the decisive key for the structural and compositional evolution. In a batch of synthetic materials, MnO/MnS with bivalent states of manganese performs best as expected. Furthermore, NaPF6 in DEGDME is taken to replace the traditional carbonate electrolyte which is prone to produce unstable SEI film and leading to polarization. A series of characterization methods confirm that the energy barrier of charge transfer at the interface between electrolyte and electrode is the main factor determining the electrochemical characteristics of the interface and thus the energy storage performance. Ultimately, carbon dots (CDS) with rich functional groups are introduced in an effort to further promote the conductivity of MnO/MnS, and the in-situ generated C-S-Mn bond enhances electrochemical kinetic behaviors. This work provides an approach for designing manganese-based materials and the selection of electrolytes in sodium energy storage.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The authors do not have permission to share data.

ce:section.ce:para: Nowadays, the rising sharp social demand for energy and the increasingly severe environmental problems are two major issues of global common concern with the continuous development of economy. Lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) have been widely popularized in various electronic devices owing to their advantages of high energy density, low pollution, and no memory effect [1]. However, the scarce resources of lithium and its high price greatly limit the development of LIBs. So it is urgent to develop a new energy storage device that can replace LIBs [2]. Sodium-ion batteries (SIBs) are promising alternatives to LIBs by virtue of low cost, abundant resources, and sustainability [3].

Exploring low-cost and long cycle life anode material is the critical problem to the development of SIBs. Transition metal oxides (TMOs), dichalcogenides (TMDs), and transition metal selenides, such as MoS2 [4], Fe2O3 [5], TiO2 [6], SnO [7], GeS2 [8], Cu2-xSe [9,10], and so on, have been widely focused due to their high theoretical specific capacity. And compared with other TMOs and transition metal selenides, manganese-based materials possess various advantages, as it exhibits rich resources, low price, and high theoretical capacity [11]. However, these materials suffer from low electrical conductivity and structural collapse in the cycling process. Carbon material coating modification [12–14] and nanocrystallization [11] are extensively investigated to enhance the performance of manganese-based anode. While, these modification methods have common issues, like the fragility of nanostructures and the low initial coulombic efficiency (ICE) of carbon modified materials. Furthermore, the heterogeneous structures of transition metals have been extensively studied in recent years, owing to their polyvalence states and crystal structures [15–17]. The construction of two-phase composites is an effective means to improve the property of multivalent materials. Unfortunately, there are many experimental methods for synthesizing heterojunction [18–20], but all of them have long reaction period, poor safety, and non-visibility of the synthesis. Hence, we propose a green and high efficiency alternating voltage induced electrochemical synthesis (AVIES) approach. As is well known, on account of ultrasmall size, abundant surface functional groups, and non-toxicity, carbon dots (CDS) are sparkling in various fields [21]. In energy storage, many studies have revealed that the CDS is a considerable material to enhance the cyclic performance of TMOs and TMDs [22,23]. Consequently, CDS have a far-reaching future in the modification of electrode materials.

In addition to modifying the anode material, optimizing the electrolyte is also an efficient and environmentally friendly method. Recent studies have proven the influence of electrolytes on anodes [24–26]. And it has been found that different electrolytes reveal different results on the performance of the same material. Wang et al. [24] reported a stable cycle life through an encoding, N, N ′-bis(glycinyl) naphthalene Diimide, with ether-based electrolytes. Yin et al. [27] demonstrated the CoS2 has a long cycle life in DEGDME with a high capacity retention rate of 94.8% even after 1500 cycles. It is clear that the ether-based electrolyte is beneficial to obtain better electrochemical performance of SIBs. However, the influence of electrolytes on manganese-based electrode performance has been virtually ignored.

In this work, we utilize alternating current to acquire different manganese valence states from changing atmosphere. Surprisingly, just because of the various valence, two composite structures MnO/MnS and Mn3O4/MnS are successfully compounded after undergoing radically disparate phases and structural evolution during the calcination. MnO/MnS with appropriate valence state shows the best performance in sodium storage. This unique structure makes up for the shortcomings of a single material, making full use of better ionic conductivity and kinetic properties of MnS compared with oxides. So expansion problem of single manganese-based material in the cycling process is alleviated through the coordination between manganese oxide and MnS. Then we investigate MnO/MnS as an anode electrode in NaPF6 in DEGDME electrolyte, predictably manifesting better ICE, capacity retention rate, and rate performance, in contrast to those coupled with other kinds of electrolytes. Combined with its energy storage dynamics in different electrolytes and XPS technology in-depth analysis about SEI composition. It is demonstrated that the chemical composition of SEI films formed in different electrolytes is particularly critical to the sodiation dynamics and energy storage performance. Moreover, MnO/MnS is further modified with CDS on the basis of optimizing the electrolyte. In-situ generated C-S-Mn bond effectively promotes the electronic conductivity and expedites ion transmission, therefore enhancing electrochemical kinetic behaviors.
The synthesis of CDS was conducted through a process in our previous report [28].

Alternating voltage electrochemical method was used to form a two-electrode system with 150 mL 1 mol L−1 Na2S solution as electrolyte and two manganese metal plates as electrodes. Before the reaction started, the AC voltage was set to 10.0 V and the frequency was set to 50 Hz, so that both electrodes had the same submerged area. During the reaction process, white powder precipitates were observed to peel off from the surface of the manganese electrodes, accompanied by a large number of bubbles, and the electrolyte gradually turned orange-yellow. After reaction for 2 h, the grey precipitate was washed with distilled water to pH = 7, and then vacuum dried at 60 °C for 12 h to obtain Mn(OH)2/S.
The precursor Mn(OH)2/S was heated up to 400 °C at 5 °C/min in argon atmosphere and hold for 2 h to obtain MnO/MnS.

The grayish precipitate was placed under air drying at 60 °C for 12 h to obtain Mn3O4/S. Then, under the same conditions, the Mn3O4/S is heated up to 400 °C at 5 °C/min in argon atmosphere and hold for 2 h to obtain Mn3O4/MnS.
The other conditions were consistent with MnO/MnS except that CDS was added in the electrolyte in advance in the AVIES method. And the precursor is named as Mn(OH)2/S@CDS.

We changed the Na2S electrolyte to NaCl in the alternating voltage electrochemical process. After reaction for 2 h, the product of AVIES process o-Mn3O4 was obtained by vacuum drying at 60 °C. Then the sample Mn3O4 was acquired through the same calcination process described above.

The crystal structures of samples were identified on Powder x-ray diffraction (Bruke XRD advance). The morphology and microstructure were characterized by scanning electron microscope (SEM Phenom XL G2) and transmission electron microscope (TEM, JEM-2100). Fourier-transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were recorded on a Nicolet 6700. Thermogravimetric analyses (TGA) were performed on a differential scanning calorimeter (TGA DSC1 1600HT) within a temperature range of 30–800 °C. BET method was carried out to investigate the specific surface. Raman spectra were recorded on a Renishaw inVia. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra were measured by Thermo Fisher ESCALAB 250Xi.

The sodium storage properties of CR2016 coin battery were studied. The electrodes were composed of active materials, acetylene black (super P), and carboxymethyl cellulose (CMC) with a mass ratio of 70:15:15. The uniform slurry was coated on Cu foil current collector and dried at 80 °C for 12 h in a vacuum. The sodium metal was used as the counter electrode and glass fiber as the separator. The electrolyte used was 1 M NaPF6 in DEGDME. The electrolytes for comparison were 1 M NaCF3SO3 in DEGDME and 1 M NaPF6 in EC/DMC/FEC (1:1:0.05). The assembly process of the half-cell was performed in the argon-filled glovebox. Charge-discharge measurements were performed on a Neware battery test system within a voltage window of 0.01–3 V. Electrochemical impedance spectra (EIS) and CV curves were recorded on a CHI660C electrochemical workstation.

In this study, we first adopt a new alternating voltage electrochemical method. The Na2S solution is employed as electrolyte, and two manganese metal sheets are applied as electrodes. After initiating the response, manganese undergoes a rapid redox reaction in Na2S electrolyte under the action of alternating current and finally forms stable Mn(OH)2. Thanks to the reducibility of S2−, most of the sulfur produced in the reaction react with Na2S to form Na2Sx, but a small part of sulfur is uniformly intercalated into Mn(OH)2 under the action of alternating current. The diffraction peaks of the precursor's X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern (Fig. S1) can accurately correspond to Mn(OH)2 (PDF#73-1604) and S (PDF#08-0247). In the above calcination course, elemental S is reoxidized to S2− and SO2 at the same time. What is interesting is that the repulsive force between the manganese in the bivalent state and the S atom in the state of S2− is the least, forming the γ-MnS with the structure of wurtzite [29]. The XRD pattern of MnO/MnS shown in Fig. 1 b presents characteristic diffraction peaks of MnO (PDF#75-0626) and MnS (PDF#65-3413), and no other impurity peaks. It reveals the formation of the two-phase structure similar to the heterostructure. In addition, because Mn(OH)2 is easily oxidized, Mn3O4/S composites can be gained through treating Mn(OH)2/S at 60 °C in an air atmosphere. Mn3O4/S displays diffraction peaks which are characteristic of Mn3O4 (PDF#75-1560) and S (PDF#08-0247) in the XRD pattern (Fig. S1). Indicating that Mn(OH)2 completely converts into Mn3O4 during this process, while the structure of S is not affected. Afterward, Mn3O4/MnS can be attained through the same calcination process. The above two redox courses exhibit below:

Amusingly, from the XRD pattern of Mn3O4/MnS (Fig. S2), it can be seen that α-MnS is gained after calcining Mn3O4/S, but γ-MnS is transformed from Mn(OH)2/S, suggesting the generated conversion reactions during calcination are different. For α-MnS, Mn2+ and six S2− build the [MnS6] octahedron, while for γ-MnS, the [MnS4] tetrahedron is formed. This results in completely different electron distributions and bond properties between the two materials, and thus slightly different binding energies [30]. The effect of these two different structures on the electrochemical properties will be discussed later. Besides, for comparison, the Na2S electrolyte in the first step change to NaCl, and pure Mn3O4 is acquired through the same steps. XRD patterns of precursor o-Mn3O4 and roasted Mn3O4 are demonstrated in Fig. S3. The reaction formula is as follows:
(6)

Finally, so as to introduce CDS into the system, the CDS are uniformly dispersed in the Na2S electrolyte in advance. Because CDS contain a variety of oxygen-containing functional groups, it is uniformly dispersed in the aqueous solution. During the AVIES process, the manganese metal is subjected to both anodic and cathodic currents eventually forming a stable Mn(OH)2/S in the Na2S electrolyte system, and then as the crystal size increases and the push of bubbles, the particle clusters on the electrode surface fall off in the electrolyte and are then adsorbed with the CDS, forming Mn(OH)2/S@CDS. Fig. S1 showcases all diffraction peaks of the XRD pattern of the precursor Mn(OH)2/S@CDS accurately to the Mn(OH)2/S. And as the XRD pattern of MnO/MnS@CDS analyzed in Fig. 1b, its diffraction peaks are also consistent with MnO/MnS. It is worth mentioning that MnO/MnS@CDS have the lower degree of crystalline compared with MnO/MnS. This can be attributed to the amorphous carbon formed by the calcination of the CDS [15,31].

Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) test (Fig. 1c) probe into the weight loss of Mn3O4/MnS, MnO/MnS, and MnO/MnS@CDS in air from room temperature to 800 °C. The result can be further analyzed to verify the material composition. First, the weight loss of three materials below 150 °C is related to the water of crystallization. Then at 300 °C, the weight gain starts owing to MnS being oxidized to MnSO4. In particular, for MnO/MnS and MnO/MnS@CDS, MnO is gradually oxidized to Mn3O4 when the curve starts at 345 °C. It is worth noting that because the carbon begins to be gradually oxidized to CO2 at around 345 °C, the weight gain of MnO/MnS@CDS at 345–400 °C is obviously less. Therefore, from this comparison of the weight increase between MnO/MnS and MnO/MnS@CDS in this temperature range, it can be seen that the carbon content of MnO/MnS@CDS is 6%. Finally, all three begin to lose weight from 400 °C to 800 °C, corresponding to the decomposition of MnSO4 into Mn3O4 [13,32]. The inset in Fig. 1c demonstrates the XRD patterns of three heterojunction materials at the end of the TGA test. Observing three samples are all Mn3O4 and MnSO4 mixtures when heated to 800 °C in air atmosphere, which verifies the above analysis. Fig. S5 reveals the TGA curve of Mn3O4 together with the XRD results, which can assist in understanding the results of weight loss after oxidation of Mn3O4 to Mn2O3. In the FT-IR spectra of MnO/MnS@CDS (Fig. 1e), except for the -OH, C=C, and C-O bonds from CDS, the signals of C-O-S (953 cm−1), and C-S (1400 cm−1) for MnO/MnS@CDS imply the successful formation of heterostructure structure MnO/MnS and hybridization of MnO/MnS with CDS [2,33]. Additionally, the Raman spectra of MnO/MnS and MnO/MnS@CDS are illustrated in Fig. 1d. The peak located at 649 cm−1 belongs to the vibration band of MnO. This result is in agreement with the XRD pattern. And the intensity ratio of the D band (defect vibration) to the G band (graphical carbon vibration) is 0.71, which manifests that CDS has been successfully embedded in MnO/MnS and its graphitization degree is high [14].

The chemical compositions of MnO/MnS and MnO/MnS@CDS are investigated via X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). The peaks of Mn, O, S, and C can be clearly observed as the full XPS spectra of MnO/MnS and MnO/MnS@CDS displayed in Fig. 2 a. The proportion of MnO and MnS in MnO/MnS can be explained by the proportion of the two elements of XPS. It can be seen that the contribution of MnO and MnS to the capacity is 65% and 35% respectively. Mn 2p spectrum peaks (Fig. 2b) at binding of 641.3 eV and 653.3 eV are corresponded to Mn 2p3/2 and Mn 2p1/2, respectively [34]. It is noteworthy that the signal at 642.3 eV in Fig. 2c comes from the C-S-Mn bond formed by MnS and CDS [11]. Fig. 2d–f collectively demonstrate the high-resolution XPS spectra of the S 2p, O 1s, and C 1s peaks of MnO/MnS@CDS. Fig. 2d reveals the high-resolution S 2p peaks, which are assigned to C-S-C (164.2 eV), SOX (168 eV), and C-S-Mn (161.6 eV), respectively [32,35]. The O 1s peaks can be divided into four peaks at 529.8, 530.4 eV, 532 eV, and 533.3 eV, corresponding to S-O, Mn-O, C=O, and C-O bonds, respectively [33,36]. Eventually, in the high-resolution XPS spectra of C1s, except for the peaks from C-C (284.8 eV), C=C (285.6 eV), and O-C=O (291 eV), there are C-S and C-S-Mn bonds at 286.1, 287 eV, echoing the previous FT-IR. Mn 2p and S 2p peaks successfully prove that CDS are successfully doped into MnO/MnS, which is beneficial to the kinetics of the reaction in the cycle.

Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM) are utilized to observe the microscopic morphology and internal composition of materials. The SEM image of the precursor Mn(OH)2/S@CDS (Fig. S6c) manifests that its particles are significantly more dispersed than those of Mn3O4/S and Mn(OH)2/S (Figs. S6a and b). CDS act as the surfactant to enhance conductivity of the system in the AVIES process, which accelerates the process of powder peeling off the electrode and makes the particles more dispersed. It is discernible that structures of Mn3O4/MnS, MnO/MnS, and MnO/MnS@CDS are composed of uniformly dispersed irregular spherical particles (Figs. S6e, 3a, and 3b). However, compared with the agglomeration shown by the SEM image of Mn3O4 (Fig. S6d), the dispersions of the above three materials are significantly more uniform. This is for the reason when S reacts with manganese oxides in calcination process, the volatilization of the generated SO2 leads to the formation of small particles, thus avoiding the agglomeration like Mn3O4. The formation of this structure is also more conducive to the effective penetration of the electrolyte and the rapid entry of ions. But the results also show that compared with MnO/MnS nanoparticles, MnO/MnS@CDS nanoparticle is more agglomerated, which is caused by the carbon coating formed by the pyrolysis of CDS in Ar atmosphere. The aggregation of these two nanoparticles is caused by annealing [35]. Additionally, in Fig. 3 d, it is easy to find the obvious periodic lattice fringes with an interlayer distance of 0.153 nm and 0.257 nm, which are assigned to the (202) plane of MnS (PDF#65-3413) and (111) plane of MnO (PDF#75-0626). This is consistent with XRD results and confirm the presence of heterojunction. What’ more, the CDS formed carbon film on the edge of the particles after calcination. We can observe chaotic amorphous lattice fringe at the edges corresponding to carbon. This coated carbon film plays roles in extending productivity and reducing volume expansion to ensure good cycle and rate performance. The element mapping images (Fig. 3e and f) verify the distribution of Mn, O, S, and C in MnO/MnS@CDS, observing these four elements are uniformly distributed in the structure.

The electrochemical properties of each material are tested through assembling a CR2016-type coin cell, and the electrolyte used was NaPF6 in DEGDME. Fig. 4 a displays CV curves of MnO/MnS at 0.2 mV s−1 in the voltage range of 0.01–3 V. In the first cycle of the cathodic scan, the weak reduction peaks at 1.83 and 1.48 V are associated with the sodium ions insertion reaction, and peaks at 0.68 and 0.36 V correspond to the formation of solid electrolyte interface (SEI), the reduction of Mn(II), and the formation of Na-S compounds (MnS + 2Na+ + 2e− → Mn + Na2S, MnO + 2Na+ +2e−→ Mn + Na2O) [16,32,37]. And three oxidation peaks located at 1.58V (reversed conversion reaction, Mn + Na2O → MnO + 2Na+ +2e−, Mn + Na2S → MnS + 2Na+ +2e−), 1.95 and 2.12V (de-insertion reaction) in the first anode scan. In the following second cycles, the CV curve changes somewhat, first the reduction peak at 0.68 V is disappeared, and the oxidation peak at 1.98 V become weaker. While the peak at 0.36 V remains the reduction of Mn (Ⅱ) is reversible, and reduction peaks such as 0.74 V, 1.09 V, and 1.53 V appear, attributing to the sodium ions insertion process [16]. And in the subsequent cycling process, the peaks at 1.95 and 1.09 V also gradually disappear, explaining the reason why the specific capacity decreases faster in the early cycle. Besides, the first three CV curves of Mn3O4/MnS (Fig. S7a) are similar to those of MnO/MnS, demonstrating their electrochemical processes are roughly the same. However, it is worth mentioning that transformation reactions of Mn3O4/MnS are Mn3O4 + 8Na+ +8e− → 3Mn + 4Na2O, and MnS + 2Na+ + 2e− → Mn + Na2S, which are different from MnO/MnS. Afterward, their sodium storage performance is given in Fig. 4b. MnO/MnS delivers highly initial reversible capacities of 571 and 533 mAh g−1 at 0.1 A g−1, and the initial discharge/charge specific capacities of Mn3O4/MnS also reach 339 and 315 mAh g−1. But single-phase material Mn3O4 due to the poor conductivity problem, only possesses the extremely low discharge/charge specific capacity of 96 and 88 mAh g−1. Meanwhile, the formation of the SEI layer, the sodiation process, and the decomposition of the electrolyte during the electrochemical process will lead to irreversible capacity loss during the initial cycling process [38]. Therefore, the reversible specific capacities of MnO/MnS and Mn3O4/MnS are ultimately 284 and 253 mAh g−1 after 100 cycles. This consequence confirms that the formation of a two-phase structure can significantly alleviate the disadvantages of raw materials and significantly improve the electrochemical performance. Of note, although the specific capacity of MnO/MnS is higher than that of Mn3O4/MnS, its cycle stability is slightly worse, reflecting the cycle process as a result of the electrode structure. It is noted that metastable γ-MnS have a lower energy barrier, conducive to ionic diffusion and electrochemical reactions of highly reversible phase transitions to achieve higher specific volumes, but from the energetics point of view, γ-MnS have a higher Gibbs free energy and larger unit cell volume, it is more unstable than α-MnS [16,30]. Why the specific capacity of Mn3O4/MnS increased a little later, is attributed to the fact that it was not effectively activated and not fully contacted with the electrolyte during the initial cycle [15]. Fig. 4c further compares their rate performance. At current densities of 0.05, 0.1, 0.2, 0.5, and 1 A g−1, the reversible specific capacities of Mn3O4 are only 100, 96, 89, 75, and 64 mAh g−1, while the reversible specific capacities of Mn3O4/MnS get to 292, 229, 188, 151, and 114 mAh g−1, and MnO/MnS reversible specific capacities of 392, 338, 289, 171, and 135 mAh g−1 are delivered. At each current density, the specific capacity comparison is MnO/MnS > Mn3O4/MnS ≫ Mn3O4. The two-phase structure exhibits better rate performance than Mn3O4.

To further analyze the argument for better electrochemical performance of MnO/MnS and Mn3O4/MnS, the kinetic mechanism is probed by CV curves at 0.2–1 mV s−1. At present, the following formula (7) is often utilized to calculate and analyze:
(7)
i = a
v b

Among them, i represents the peak current, v represents the scan rate, a and b are adjustable parameters. When the b value is close to 0.5, it mainly represents ion diffusion; while the b value is close to 1, it is mainly controlled by capacitance [39]. The b value can be received from the slope of the log(i) versus log(v) curve. Fig. 4d depicts the CV curves of MnO/MnS at different scan rates. And the fitting curves shown in Fig. 4e can be obtained on the basis of their peak currents and scan rates. According to the fitting results, the b values of the six redox peaks O1, O2, O3, R1, R2, and R3 are 0.7344, 0.8883, 0.5520, 0.6780, 0.6723, and 1.0949, revealing pseudocapacitive behavior synergistically dominate the storage process, which is beneficial to the rapid ion diffusion [40]. For Mn3O4/MnS, Figs. S7b and c are the CV of different scan rates and fitting curves. Similarly, six peaks have b values of 0.5398, 0.7083, 0.4949, 0.6007, 0.5240, and 0.8085, manifesting that its electrochemical behavior is also controlled by ion diffusion and capacitive behavior, but the ion diffusion take hold. In addition, in accordance with the formula i(V) = k1V + k2V1/2, i(V) is the current at a fixed potential, k1V and k2V1/2 represent the contribution of capacity and ionic diffusion [2]. As Fig. 4f presented, MnO/MnS capacitance contribution increases from 62.9% to 79.4% with the scan rate increasing from 0.2 mV s−1 to 1 mV s−1. These outcomes reflect that the capacitive behavior plays an important role in sodium storage, and the high proportion of capacitive behavior is conducive to the high-speed transport of sodium ions, resulting in superior electrochemical performance.

Previously, the electrolyte used to assemble coin cell to discuss material properties is NaPF6 in DEGDME. As is known to all, different electrolytes play different roles in the electrochemical performance of anode [25,41–45]. Hence electrochemical properties of the MnO/MnS electrode in three diverse electrolytes (NaPF6 in DEGDME, NaCF3SO3 in DEGDME, and NaPF6 in EC-DMC-5% FEC) are tested utilizing 2016 coin-type cells. Fig. 5 a describes CV curves of MnO/MnS in the first cycle under three electrolytes at 0.2 mV s−1. It can be found that oxidation peaks of electrolytes NaPF6 in DEGDME, NaCF3SO3 in DEGDME, and NaPF6 in EC-DCM-5% FEC, move to the direction of low potential in turn, while reduction peaks shift to the direction of high potential. These confirm the small polarization problem of the material in the electrolyte (NaPF6 in DEGDME) [16]. What's more, the three oxidation peaks in the electrolyte (NaPF6 in DEGDME) are stronger than those of the other two electrolytes, depicting that the material with this electrolyte has relatively more active sites when cycling and can better exert its potential. Figs. S10a and b show CV curves of the same material MnO/MnS in electrolytes (NaCF3SO3 in DEGDME and NaPF6 in EC-DMC-5% FEC) when the scan rate increases from 0.2 mV s−1 to 1 mV s−1. The redox peaks of the material are clearly observed to gradually decreased or even disappeared in the electrolyte (NaPF6 in EC-DMC-5% FEC), which agree with the poor electrochemical performance of manganese-based materials in ester electrolyte. Fig. 5b exhibits the charge-discharge curves in the first cycle under the three electrolytes of MnO/MnS, matching the CV curve (Fig. 5a) well. It should be point out that ICE of the electrolytes NaPF6 in DEGDME, NaCF3SO3 in DEGDME, and NaPF6 in EC-DMC-5% FEC are 93.3%, 78.5%, and 58.0%, respectively. The material demonstrates ultra-high ICE in electrolyte NaPF6 in DEGDME and inferior in ester electrolytes. As evidenced in Fig. 5c and d, MnO/MnS combined with the electrolyte (NaPF6 in DEGDME) displays the highest performance. Fig. 5e and f presents EIS diagrams of the pristine and cycled states of three electrolytes, respectively. This semicircle in high-frequency region is referred to the charge-transfer resistances (Rct) between the electrodes and electrolyte. And the obviously lower Rct after 14 cycles indicates that this charge transport kinetic is improved. This can be attribute to the SEI film which could reduce energy barrier at interface [23]. Furthermore, this patterning implies that the impedance reflecting charge transfer and ion diffusion in ether electrolyte is much smaller than that in ester electrolyte. Low impedance is universally beneficial to electrochemical reactions, which is accurately reflected through the small potential polarization.

To have insights into the relationship between electrolyte and SEI layer, the chemical property of the SEI layer is explored by XPS. It is obvious from Fig. 5c, in the cycling process, the specific capacity of the MnO@MnS first decreases rapidly and then gradually tends to stabilize. In order to further discuss the influence of ether electrolyte on SEI film, the cycling process is divided into three parts: pristine, State-i which decreases faster, and State-ii which tends to be stable. XPS technology is performed on the anode electrodes in these three states. Both State-i and State-ii are tested in the fully charged state of 3V. First of all, as shown in Fig. 6 a, d, and g, the two peaks of Mn 2p spectrum are slightly redshifted along with the cycling process, which can be explained by more electrons are transferred from Mn to S under the driving force of the interaction between the formed SEI film and the sample. In the F1s spectrum (Fig. 6b, e, and h), the Na-F peak gradually appears with the cycling progress, suggesting an increase inorganic components with low Na+ diffusion activation energy in the SEI film [41]. Furthermore, Fig. 6c, f, and i analyze the O 1s spectrum of the electrodes surface analysis in three states, indicating SEI film is mainly composed of Na2CO3, R (CO)O-Na, and R-ONa, which are among the reduction products of DEGDME. It is worth mentioning that the proportion of R-ONa and Na2CO3 in the SEI film component in State-ii increases significantly. The existence of R-ONa facilitates the transport of Na+ and reduce the energy barrier for Na+ diffusion, which is favorable to stability of the electrode [44]. And the increase in the content of Na2CO3 further proves that the proportion of inorganic components in SEI film is aggrandizing. Obviously, the reasons why manganese-based materials exhibit better performance in NaPF6 in DMEDEG electrolyte are R-ONa and the presence of inorganic components (NaF, Na2CO3) in SEI film. And with the increase of cycle numbers, the cycling curve is prone to be steady due to the proportion of these components in SEI film increase. In addition, as presented in Fig. S11, in the State-ii stage, S2 2− is formed on the electrode surface. Early studies have verified that Na2Sn has an irreversible reaction with EC/DMC, but it is stable in DEGDME. This explains the low initial coulombic efficiency and rapid capacity decay of the material in EC/DME based electrolyte [27].

As summarized in Table S1, although the electrochemical performance of MnO/MnS has been greatly improved over other reported manganese-based materials, its stability and rate performance still could be enhanced. CDS have been repeatedly confirmed in the field of electrochemistry thanks to their rich functional groups and other advantages. So that this work acquires MnO/MnS@CDS via adding CDS in advance to the electrolyte and the same follow-up operation. CDS are absorbed in the precursor under the help of alternating current, and then carbonized during calcination to form carbon coated on the manganese material and C-S-Mn bond with manganese These could improve its conductivity and reduce volume expansion during cycling to enhance electrochemical kinetic behavior and rate performance. It is also assembled as an anode electrode material into CR2016 half cells to measure its electrochemical performance. Fig. 7 a displays several peaks in the CV curve of MnO/MnS@CDS for the first three cycles under the voltage range of 0.01–3 V, in general agreement with the peaks of the CV curve for MnO/MnS given in Fig. 4a, concluding the introduction of CDS has no effect on the electrochemical behavior. The charge-discharge cycle curve of doped CDS material at 0.1 A g−1 (Fig. 7b) can be clearly observed that the specific capacity is somewhat reduced compared with MnO/MnS. Nonetheless, the specific capacity of MnO/MnS@CDS remains at 263 mAh g−1 after 100 cycles, and its retention rate is increased by 18.1% compared with no CDS. The certain and appropriate amount of carbon matrix can render MnO/MnS@CDS stable for cycling capacity. Moreover, the contrast of rate performance of the two materials is evaluated in Fig. 7c. It can be seen that when current densities are 0.5 and 1 A g−1, specific capacities of MnO/MnS@CDS are 198 and 167 mAh g−1, which are higher than 171 and 134 mAh g−1 of MnO/MnS. It further verifies that the formation of C-S-Mn bond boosts the rate performance of the material. The reaction kinetics of MnO/MnS@CDS is exhibited in Fig. 7d–f. The low degree of polarization is substantiated through the similar contours demonstrated in CV curves of 0.2–1 mV s−1 (Fig. 7d). And fitting curves in Fig. 7e present the redox peak b values at the 6 locations are 1.082, 0.746, 0.596, 0.922, 0.716, and 1.148, illustrating that the electrochemical behavior of MnO/MnS@CDS is controlled by the synergistic effect of faraday diffusion and pseudocapacitor behavior. But the main component is pseudocapacitor behavior. Furthermore, when the scanning speed aggrandizes from 0.2 mV s−1 to 1 mV s−1 (Fig. 7f), the proportion of pseudocapacitor behavior increases from 77.3% to 88.9%. The above reflects the reaction kinetics of MnO/MnS@CDS mainly based on pseudocapacitance behavior, which guarantees great performance.

In summary, an innovative alternating voltage approach is adopted to gain the bivalent and quadrivalent states of manganese with different atmosphere treatments, and subsequently get expected hetero-phase Mn3O4/MnS and MnO/MnS after simple calcination. It has been demonstrated that valence states determine the evolution and transformation mechanism of manganese, resulting in sodium energy storage. The structural evolutionary mechanism of the above processes is systematically studied by structural characterization technique, and it is proved that the two-phase structure effectively combines the advantages of the two structure and alleviates the volume expansion of manganese-based materials. So that in the electrolyte NaPF6 in DEGDME, the sodium storage performance of MnO/MnS (284 mAh g−1 at 0.1 A g−1 after 100 cycles) and Mn3O4/MnS (253 mAh g−1 at 0.1A g−1 after 100 cycles) is superior to that of Mn3O4. In addition, MnO/MnS in the electrolyte NaPF6 in DEGDME shows the superlative electrochemical performance such as ICE and capacity retention, both of which are much better than in the EC/DMC-based ester electrolyte. XPS spectra reveal that the chemical properties and composition distribution of the SEI formed from NaPF6 in DEGDME are strongly related to charge transfer, and that the formation of organic species (R-ONa) in ether electrolytes together with inorganic species (Na2CO3 and NaF) is conducive to reaction kinetics. We have also shown that the content of chemical components with low Na+ diffusion energy barriers are increased gradually as the cycle process progressed, which is highly beneficial to fast charge storage and long-term stability. Finally, we further successfully gain MnS/MnO@CDS through adding CDS with rich functional groups and high conductivity into the electrolyte. The formation of C-S-Mn bond increases the pseudocapacitive behavior, which is profitable to elevate the rate performance (167 mAh g−1 at 1 A g−1) and stability (18.1% capacity retention rate improvement). This work provides a reference for conversion mechanism and modification of manganese-based materials, and pave a path for selecting electrolyte for manganese-based materials in SIBs.
